query: What is the difference between relative, absolute, and fixed positioning?

competitors.label: CSS positioned layout
competitors.selectionReason: This MDN module is the canonical positioned-layout reference for relative, absolute, fixed, sticky, offsets, and stacking behavior.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on how positioned layout works and how positioning interacts with painting and stacking behavior.
theirGaps: It is broader than the exact compare question and less focused on common production failures like the wrong containing block or fixed overlays covering content.

realWorldUseCases:
Practical scenario
Pin a badge to a card corner and a header to the top on scroll.

Common pitfalls
  • Absolute elements escaping because the parent isn’t positioned.
  • Fixed elements causing overlay or z-index conflicts.
  • Ignoring mobile viewport and safe areas.
Trade-off or test tip
Absolute positioning is simple but brittle. Test on scroll, resize, and mobile viewports.
followUpConfusion: Fixed elements always align with the viewport unless inside a transformed parent (e.g., transform: translateZ(0) creates a new stacking context).

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the positioned-layout model well, but it is less centered on one compact badge-plus-header use case and the bugs it creates.
actionableExamples: The docs cover the positioning module and properties thoroughly, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: The module covers stacking and offsets, but our page is more direct about transformed parents, overlay conflicts, and mobile viewport caveats that often appear as follow-up questions.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter containing-block snippet that shows a child escaping before the parent gets positioned.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the authoritative positioned-layout reference, but our page is sharper as an interview answer because it ties the concepts to concrete UI bugs and includes the transformed-parent and safe-area follow-ups teams miss.

competitors.label: CSS Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's CSS interview bank is directly relevant and explicitly includes a positioned-elements question in its CSS fundamentals set.
theirStrengths: The bank covers positioning as a standard CSS interview topic and provides broad interview-style practice.
theirGaps: The hub page is not dedicated to relative/absolute/fixed trade-offs or to the common scroll and overlay bugs they create.

realWorldUseCases:
Use Cases
  • relative: fine-tune an element’s position for minor alignment.
  • absolute: create dropdown menus or tooltips detached from normal layout flow.
  • fixed: keep navigation bars or chat buttons visible during scrolling.
followUpConfusion: Nested absolute elements look for the nearest ancestor with position: relative or absolute to align against.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd clearly covers CSS fundamentals, but the landing page is less explicit about containing-block bugs, transformed parents, and mobile overlay concerns.
actionableExamples: Because the bank includes detailed explanations, the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: The hub is less focused on exactly why absolute positioning appears to 'escape' and how fixed positioning changes inside transformed ancestors.

actionableExamples: Keep one reduced card-badge example with the positioned parent highlighted more explicitly.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is a useful CSS interview hub, but our page is more focused on the exact positioning decisions and the follow-up bugs interviewers often ask about next.

competitors.label: Absolute, Relative, Fixed Positioning: How Do They Differ?
competitors.selectionReason: This CSS-Tricks article is a direct topic match and is the strongest tutorial comparator for relative, absolute, and fixed positioning.
theirStrengths: The article is directly on-topic and clearly explains that fixed positions to the viewport and absolute is removed from normal flow.
theirGaps: It is lighter on transformed-parent behavior, safe areas, and the exact UI trade-offs around overlays and scroll.

realWorldUseCases: Absolute elements escaping because the parent isn’t positioned.
followUpConfusion: Fixed elements always align with the viewport unless inside a transformed parent (e.g., transform: translateZ(0) creates a new stacking context).

realWorldUseCases: CSS-Tricks explains that overuse or improper use of absolute positioning can limit layout flexibility and that fixed elements can overlap content.
actionableExamples: It is a direct explainer with concrete examples, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: The article is less explicit about transformed ancestors, mobile safe areas, and z-index debugging than our page.

actionableExamples: Add one even shorter scroll demo snippet that isolates fixed overlap from absolute anchoring.

competitors.winReason: CSS-Tricks is a strong direct comparator, but our page goes further on the real-world failure modes: escaped absolute children, transformed parents affecting fixed positioning, and the scroll-safe testing checklist.
